technical_knowledge.q8_complex_feature: I built a real-time booking and availability system for sports courts using Flutter and BLoC. The feature handled dynamic time slots, live updates, and user interactions with complex validation logic, ensuring that selected slots were always in sync with backend data. One challenge was managing state consistency across multiple widgets while avoiding unnecessary rebuilds, which I solved using scoped BlocBuilders and optimized state updates. I also implemented responsive UI and smooth UX with horizontal scrolling and selectable time tables. I was satisfied with the result because it combined performance, clean architecture, and real-time interaction effectively.
technical_knowledge.q9_stateful_vs_bloc:
I decide based on the complexity and scope of the state. If the feature involves simple, local UI state (like toggles, animations, or form inputs), I use StatefulWidget because it’s lightweight and faster to implement. However, if the state needs to be shared across multiple widgets, involves business logic, API calls, or requires scalability and testability, I use BLoC.

I also consider maintainability — for features expected to grow or change over time, BLoC provides better separation of concerns and cleaner architecture. In many cases, I combine both: StatefulWidget for UI-related state and BLoC for handling business logic and data flow.
technical_knowledge.q10_code_output:
4
true

Why list1.length prints 4:
final list2 = list1 does not copy the list. It copies the reference — both variables point to the exact same list object in memory.

So when you call list2.add(4), you're mutating that one shared object:

list1 never "knew" about the add — it just happens to point to the same place that changed.

Why identical() prints true:
identical(a, b) checks if two variables point to the exact same object in memory — not just equal values, but literally the same instance. Since list2 = list1 copied the reference, they are the same object, so identical returns true.
This is different from == which checks value equality:
dartfinal a = [1, 2, 3];
final b = [1, 2, 3];     // different object, same values

print(a == b);            // true  (same values)
print(identical(a, b));   // false (different objects)

To make an actual independent copy:
dartfinal list2 = List.of(list1);   // new object, copied elements
// or
final list2 = [...list1];       // spread operator, same effect
Now list2.add(4) would NOT affect list1.
technical_knowledge.q12_const_sizedbox:
The difference is related to how Flutter handles widget instantiation and rebuilds.

When using const SizedBox(), the widget is created at compile time and reused whenever possible. Flutter treats it as an immutable object and does not rebuild or recreate it during widget rebuilds, which improves performance and reduces memory usage.

On the other hand, SizedBox() (without const) is created at runtime every time the build method runs, even if its properties don’t change. This leads to unnecessary object creation and can slightly impact performance in frequently rebuilding widgets.

In short, const allows Flutter to reuse the same widget instance and skip redundant rebuild work, while non-const widgets are recreated on every build.
technical_knowledge.q13_friday_bug:
Step 1: Assess impact quickly
I first determine how critical the issue is — how many users are affected, whether it blocks core functionality, and if there’s any workaround.

Step 2: Reproduce the issue
I try to reproduce the bug locally or using logs (Crashlytics, backend logs) to clearly understand the root cause.

Step 3: Contain the problem
If possible, I apply a quick mitigation (feature flag, disabling a broken feature, or backend hotfix) to reduce user impact immediately.

Step 4: Fix the root cause
I implement a proper fix in a separate branch, keeping the change minimal and focused to reduce risk.

Step 5: Test thoroughly
I verify the fix locally and, if available, on staging — covering edge cases related to the bug.

Step 6: Release hotfix
I create a hotfix build and coordinate a fast release (App Store / Play Store or backend deployment depending on the issue).

Step 7: Monitor after release
I monitor logs, crashes, and user feedback to ensure the issue is fully resolved.

Step 8: Post-mortem
After stabilization, I document the issue, root cause, and prevention steps (tests, monitoring improvements) to avoid similar problems in the future.

how_you_work.q14_architecture_disagreement:
In a previous project, the team decided to manage most of the app state using global variables and direct API calls inside UI widgets for faster delivery. I disagreed because this approach would make the code harder to maintain, test, and scale as the app grows.

I suggested using BLoC with a clear separation between presentation, business logic, and data layers. I explained the long-term benefits, especially for handling complex features and reducing bugs. Initially, the team was concerned about the added complexity and development time.

To address that, I implemented a small feature using BLoC as a proof of concept. After seeing improved readability and easier debugging, the team gradually adopted the pattern across the project.

In the end, we reached a balanced approach where simple features used lightweight solutions, while complex ones used BLoC, which improved overall code quality and team productivity.
how_you_work.q15_staying_current:
I stay current by following official Flutter and Dart release notes, as well as the Flutter blog and documentation. I also regularly watch conference talks (like Flutter Engage and Google I/O) to understand new features and best practices.

In addition, I follow experienced developers on platforms like GitHub, Twitter, and LinkedIn, and explore open-source projects to see real-world implementations. I also experiment with new features in small side projects to understand them عمليًا before using them in production.

Finally, I make it a habit to refactor parts of my existing code when better patterns or improvements become available, ensuring my codebase stays up to date and maintainable.
how_you_work.q16_code_review:
My approach to code review focuses on both correctness and maintainability. I start by understanding the purpose of the change — what problem it solves and whether the solution is appropriate.

I look for clean architecture and separation of concerns, making sure business logic is not mixed with UI and that the code follows consistent patterns (like BLoC usage). I also check for performance issues such as unnecessary rebuilds, missing const constructors, or inefficient state handling.

In addition, I review code readability — clear naming, proper structure, and whether the code is easy for another developer to understand. I also check edge cases, error handling, and whether the changes could introduce bugs.

A good PR, in my opinion, is small, focused, well-described, and easy to review. It should include clear context (what and why), follow coding standards, and ideally be tested or at least verified with a clear explanation of how it was tested.
how_you_work.q17_ai_usage: I did use them in this task and helped  me understand code and some bugs
